plan.description: This is version 1.0 of the uBIQUity Data Management Plan for the ITSERR Project.
plan.title: ITSERR WP8 uBIQUity DMP

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

reference.label: University of Modena and Reggio Emilia
Institute of Information Science and Technologies "A. Faedo"

description.description: This is version 1.0 of the uBIQUity Data Management Plan for the ITSERR Project.
description.label: ITSERR WP8 uBIQUity DMP

descriptionTemplate.label: IOSSG Template for ITSERR
descriptionTemplate.description:
This template was inspired by the work done by the IOSSG checklist: http://wikimedia.sp.unipi.it/images/Grigliapianodigestionedatiricerca.pdf
It was created to support EU Horizon and Next Generation EU Research projects.

fieldSet.title: Provenance of data
fieldSet.description: Describe whether the data come from interviews, surveys, extracted from disciplinary archives, etc.

fieldSet.title: Type of data
fieldSet.description: Describe whether they are qualitative or quantitative.

options.label: Text docs (DOC, ODF, PDF, TXT, etc)
options.value: Text docs (DOC, ODF, PDF, TXT, etc)

options.label: Structured data (HTML, JSON, TEX, XML, RDF)
options.value: Structured data (HTML, JSON, TEX, XML, RDF)

options.label: Spreadsheet (CSV, ODS,TSV, XLS, SAS, Stata, SPSS portable)
options.value: Spreadsheet (CSV, ODS,TSV, XLS, SAS, Stata, SPSS portable)

options.label: Source code (C, CSS, JavaScript, Java, etc)
options.value: Source code (C, CSS, JavaScript, Java, etc)

fieldSet.title: Nature and formats
fieldSet.description: Describe the nature (text, images, audio, video, numerical data, etc.) .and format of the data (preferably non-proprietary format).

fieldSet.title: Amount of data
fieldSet.description: Estimate the size of the entire dataset

fieldSet.title: Requirements for software and hardware
fieldSet.description: Specify the possible use of specific software/hardware.

fieldSet.title: Data re-use and integration
fieldSet.description: In case of data reuse, declare their integration and reuse in the project.

fieldSet.title: New Dataset value
fieldSet.description: Justify the creation of the new dataset, defining its added value for the scientific community or others.

section.description: Describe the existing data or the data intended to be created, including their source, nature, and order of magnitude. Justify the creation of new datasets and their added value.
section.title: Dataset Description

section.description: Motivate the creation of new data in terms of content and quality. Describe the metadata and accompanying documentation of the datasets, with references to discipline standards, interoperability, and regulations.
section.title: Standards and Metadata

labelData.label: Description of the metadata standard(s) used
ID of the metadata standard(s) if known (i.e. http://www.dublincore.org/specifications/dublin-core/dcmi-terms/)

fieldSet.title: Metadata standards and data documentation
fieldSet.description:
Describe the type of metadata used
  1. With your own terms(e.g., Dublin Core, ADS Guides and Guidelines for metadata, DCC guide for Metadata Standard, etc.), any domain extensions, and project specifications
  2. Using eventual standards identifiers.

fieldSet.title: Who collects the data and how?
Who structures and stores the data, and how?
Who processes the data and how?
Who distributes the data and how?

section.description: Describe the methodologies for data collection and production during the research process.
section.title: Methodologies for data collection/generation

fieldSet.title: Data consistency and quality
fieldSet.description: Document how to ensure consistency and quality of the collected or produced data in terms of standards, calibration, validation, review, etc.

fieldSet.title: Disciplinary standards applied, if available
fieldSet.description: Refer to appropriate disciplinary standards, if available: in case the data types do not currently conform to any existing standards, this info might help planning for their conversion.

fieldSet.title: Metadata reusability constraints
fieldSet.description:
In case of metadata reuse, declare if there are any constraints on their reuse. As examples:
  1. Copyright and intellectual property rights: If the metadata contains information that is protected by copyright or other intellectual property rights, this could limit its reuse.
  2. Privacy and confidentiality: If the metadata includes sensitive or confidential information, such as personally identifiable information (PII), it may have restrictions on how it can be reused to protect privacy.
  3. Licenses: If the metadata is provided under a certain license, the terms of that license could impose constraints on reuse. For example, a license might require that users attribute the source of the metadata, or it might prohibit commercial use of the metadata.
  4. Data quality or completeness: If there are known issues with the quality or completeness of the metadata, this could limit its usefulness for certain purposes.
  5. Technical constraints: There could be technical constraints on metadata reuse, for example, if the metadata is in a format that is not widely supported, or if it is stored in a system that has limited access or interoperability.

fieldSet.title: Data quality assurance criteria
fieldSet.description: Describe the criteria planned to ensure data quality assurance (ex: data clearing procedures).

section.description: Describe in technical terms the processes adopted for data management, documentation, curation, and preservation. This serves as a summary for the detailed information provided in the "Dataset Description." Estimate the costs associated with data management and maintenance.
section.title: Data Management, Documentation and Curation

fieldSet.title: Storage
fieldSet.description:
Here are some aspects that you might consider when describing your storage methods in your Preservation Plan:
  1. Storage Medium: This could be local storage (such as on your own computer or on a local server), cloud storage (such as Google Drive, AWS S3, or a research data repository), or some combination of these.
  2. Storage Format: The data could be stored in raw format, as processed data, or in some kind of standard or proprietary format. If a standard format is used, this can aid in data preservation and reuse.
  3. Version Control: This is the process of tracking and managing changes to your data. It can be particularly important if your data is updated or modified over time.
  4. Data Organization: This involves structuring your data in a logical way, often in a hierarchical folder structure. Good data organization can make your data easier to navigate and understand.
  5. Metadata Storage: This involves storing metadata (data about your data) alongside your data. Metadata can provide important context that helps to make your data understandable and reusable.
  6. Data Accessibility: This includes considering who will be able to access your data, and how they will do so. This can involve setting up permissions and access controls, and making sure that the data is stored in a location that is accessible to those who need to use it.
  7. Long-Term Preservation: After the project is finished, you may need to arrange for long-term storage of your data in a data repository or archive.

fieldSet.title: Backup
Transmission
fieldSet.description:
Transmission is a critical component of data management and preservation because it is often necessary to move data to a different location for backup, to share it with other researchers, or to deposit it in a repository for long-term preservation.
When planning for data transmission, several factors need to be considered:
  • Security: The data should be transmitted in a secure way to prevent unauthorized access, alteration, or loss. This might involve using encryption, secure file transfer protocols, or other security measures.
  • Reliability: The transmission method should be reliable, to ensure that the data is not lost or corrupted during the transfer process.
  • Efficiency: The transmission method should be efficient, especially when dealing with large volumes of data. This might involve using high-speed networks, data compression techniques, or other methods to reduce the time and resources required for data transfer.
  • Accessibility: The data should be accessible to the intended recipients. This might involve considering the technical capabilities and constraints of the recipients, such as their internet bandwidth or the systems and software they have available for receiving and using the data.

fieldSet.title: Data curation in the short and medium term

section.description: Preparing a Preservation Plan that describes the methods for: 1. storage; 2. backup; 3. transmission; 4. data curation in the short and medium term, with references to practices, standards, and regulations where applicable. Security-related issues are deferred to the "Data Security..." section.
section.title: Managing, storing and curating data

fieldSet.title: Describe the long-term preservation methods of data in certified repositories
fieldSet.description: Describe the long-term preservation methods of data in certified repositories (ARCA, community-based, domain-specific, or project-specific).

fieldSet.title: Data excluded from the preservation plan
fieldSet.description: If the preservation only applies to a subset of the collected data, provide a justification for the exclusion.

fieldSet.title: Data preservation period
fieldSet.description: Declare the duration of data preservation in the repository.

fieldSet.title: Data potential deletion date
fieldSet.description: Declare the potential deletion date, if the data has a limited lifespan.

fieldSet.title: Data management costs
fieldSet.description:
Indicate the annual costs for:
- the repository
- any data preparation for archiving (costs for legal rights advice or technical solutions for conversion)
- data reuse/visualization.

page.title: Data Security and Confidentially of Potentially Disclosive Information

section.title: Data Security and Confidentially of Potentially Disclosive Information

fieldSet.title: Identify which data are of a confidential/sensitive nature
University and project standards adhesion
fieldSet.description: Formally identify the standards (e.g., ISO 27001) that the project or the university adheres to.

fieldSet.title: Main risks to data security
fieldSet.description: Describe to participants in the data collection the risks involved in terms of confidentiality and security.

fieldSet.title: Describe each data security risk's
fieldSet.description:
Specifically, for each risk, identify:
  1. Description of the risk
  2. Risk level (low, medium, high)
  3. Probability of the risk occurring (low, medium, high)
  4. Impact on the different stakeholders involved
  5. Monitoring methods
  6. Mitigation strategies
  7. Countermeasures in case of occurrence.

section.description: Specify the procedures for data collection and storage, including non-digital methods, audit procedures, the organization of responsibilities, and any permissions required (e.g., images with people or audio reproductions).
section.title: Main risks to data security

section.description:
Aterisks (*) are referring to the DMP Guidelines.
Describe the risks associated with the management of sensitive data, specifically addressing:
section.title: Ethics and Legal Compliance

fieldSet.title: Does the research involve human embryonic stem cells (hESCs)?
Does the research involve the use of human embryos?*
Does the research involve the use of human fetal tissues?
Does the research involve the participation of individuals?*
Does the research involve physical interventions on the individuals being studied?*
Does the research involve human cells or tissues?*
Does the research involve the collection and/or processing of personal data?*
Does the research involve the reprocessing of previously collected personal data (secondary use) from data sets, pre-existing sources, assembled data, or data shared with non-European countries
Does the research involve animals?*
Does the research involve the reprocessing of previously collected personal data (secondary use) from data sets, pre-existing sources, assembled data, or data shared with non-European countries?
Are there research activities conducted in countries outside Europe that can raise ethical concerns? If yes, please specify the countries involved.
Is the use of local resources anticipated?
Is there a plan to import any type of material (including personal data) from non-European countries into the European Union?*
Is there a plan to export any type of material (including personal data) from the European Union to non-European countries?
In case the research involves low-income and/or lower-middle-income countries, are there plans to take actions that will lead to benefit sharing?
Does the country's situation pose risks to those participating in the research?
Does the research involve the use of elements that could harm the environment, animals, or plants?
Does the research focus on endangered fauna and/or flora or protected areas?
Does the research involve dual-use products under Regulation 428/2009, or other products for which authorization is required?
Could the research raise concerns due to its exclusive focus on civilian applications?

section.title: Exclusive focus on civilian applications or civilian use

fieldSet.title: Could the research generate improper uses of the research outcomes?

labelData.label: Provide an URL to a report stating the ethical issues.

fieldSet.title: Are there any additional ethical issues that should be taken into consideration?

section.description: Describe which data will be shared, how, and in what manner they will be made available, including policies on repository access, data transmission, and data circulation. The response could be summarized, with a more detailed specification provided in the detailed dataset plan where relevant. This is particularly relevant for research projects that provide open access. Not to be completed (except for the "Suitable for sharing" question) for projects with closed access. In such cases, provide justification for the closed access.
section.title: Data Sharing and Access

labelData.label: complete openness or restriction to specific groups to be specified in the section "The study team’s exclusive use of the data"

fieldSet.title: Data sharing - Mode and justification
fieldSet.description: Justify the data sharing and the mode of sharing.

labelData.label: Add other licenses in use not present in the previous list

fieldSet.title: Data licensing
fieldSet.description: Declare the license(s) to which the data is bound

fieldSet.title: Declare the possibility of citation by third parties, the software and tools for reuse, and the repository in which the data will be deposited for sharing:
fieldSet.description:
a) community databases,
b) Institutional Repository: ARCA (compliant with Open Access Infrastructure for Research In Europe OpenAIRE)
c) re3data, etc.;

fieldSet.title: Non-sharing of data - declaration and context
fieldSet.description: The potential non-sharing of data should be declared and contextualized.

fieldSet.title: External users - Details the benefits of data sharing to external users
fieldSet.description: Concisely identify which external users could benefit from the data sharing and how. This may overlap with the general statement (section: Administrative Plan Details, template: Project Description) where the research benefits for the scientific community or society are typically described.

section.title: Discovery by potential users of research data

fieldSet.title: Identify the decision-makers responsible for data openness and the adopted policies.
Restrictions "ad intra"
fieldSet.description: Respond in case the data will only be accessible confidentially, possibly for a limited period to be determined. Please specify the end of the limited period of access.

section.title: The study team’s exclusive use of the data

fieldSet.title: Restrictions “ad extra”
fieldSet.description: Describe the access restrictions to the data due to confidentiality issues, agreements, privacy concerns, and intellectual property considerations. Include the use of anonymization, aggregation techniques, and embargoes (duration and conditions).

section.title: Restrictions or delays to sharing, with planned actions to limit such restrictions

fieldSet.title: Specify which external users are subject to agreements regarding data dissemination.
Data capture Responsibility
Metadata creation Responsibility
Quality assurance of data Responsibility
Data security Responsibility
Data archiving & data sharing Responsibility
Policy compliance Responsibility
Additional resources required to deliver the plan
fieldSet.description: Specify the requests, preferably accompanied by an estimate: 1) Specialist/Technical expertise, legal consulting, technical consulting (or training for existing staff) for data management and long-term archiving 2) Hardware or Software 3) Data Repositories charges.

page.title: Relevant Institutional Policies on Data Sharing and Data Security

fieldSet.title: References to University policies and applicable legislation
fieldSet.description: Es: Open Access Repository and Scientific Publications, http://www.unive.it/pag/8254/

section.title: Relevant Institutional Policies on Data Sharing and Data Security

field.textValue: Costs are managed and accounted for in the ITSERR DMP.
Data is preserved for the duration stated in the ITSERR DMP.
https://tei-c.org/; https://crosswire.org/osis/osisCore.2.1.1.xsd; https://epidoc.stoa.org/; https://openiti.org/; https://viaf.org/en; https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:Main_Page

Text Encoding Initiative (TEI); Open Scripture Information Standard (OSIS); Epigraphic Documents in TEI XML (EpiDoc); Open Islamicate Texts Initiative (OpenITI); VIAF: The Virtual International Authority File (authority file); WIKIDATA (authority file)

The uBIQUity project, which incorporates the “BI” of the Bible(s) and the “QU” of the Qurʾān in its title, investigates the sacred texts of Christianity and Islam in different environments and historical periods through two huge corpora: Greek and Latin Christian commentaries (broadly understood as written forms of exegesis) on the Bible(s) composed from the patristic age until the late Byzantine period, and classical commentaries on the Qurʾān written in Arabic (tafāsīr) from the rise of Islam until the 15th century. Intertextual references, conscious or unconscious, embedded in these ancient works function as invisible “places of memory”, making sacred texts “ubiquitous” (hence the title of the project). Users of uBIQUity software may be researches in different fields of Religious Studies interested in intertextuality within these corpora. They will be able to perform textual queries based on different semantic levels (from identical tokens to contextual similarity), run comparisons, browse texts, take notes, access their workflow, and save previous searches. The software includes various types of data visualization: parallel view, variant graph, distant reading, node-based view. This novel semantic search engine might find further applications in the research of intertextual references in different kinds of literary production.
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO
Data is licensed with a creative commons 4.0 CC BY-ND license.
Long-term preservation of data is achieved through upload in Zenodo.
Data is distributed (where applicable) by the ITSERR project, according to the ITSERR DMP.
No

Potential users of the data include researchers in Philology, History, Exegesis and Religious Studies, who may reuse the structured data for intertextual analysis within the corpora included in uBIQUity. Additionally, lexicographic and editorial projects working on historical dictionaries or critical editions could leverage the dataset to improve accuracy and consistency in textual representation. Digital Humanities and Computational Linguistics scholars could also benefit from the dataset for purposes such as training NLP models or enriching corpora. Moreover, developers of annotation tools or retrieval systems might use the data to test or adapt domain-specific functionalities. Academic and cultural institutions, including digital libraries and research infrastructures (e.g., CLARIN), may integrate the data into their collections to enhance accessibility and interoperability. Graphic and interface designers may draw inspiration from the uBIQUity’s data model and visual outputs to develop more intuitive and user-friendly designs for textual analysis tools.
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO
Low Impact
a. accidental leakage: this can occur when WP members with access to data accidentally share data with third parties.
Monitoring is ensured through periodic check of access to data.
Mitigation is achieved by ensuring that sensitive or security-relevant data is collected and stored only when necessary.
Low Risk Level
Low Occurrence Prob.
Medium Impact
b. accidental data deletion occurs when a WP member or anyone with access to the data accidentally deletes the data or makes it inaccessible (e.g. through removing access rights).
Data access is periodically checked.
Data backups and journaling file systems or cloud services mitigate the risk of accidental data deletion.
Medium Risk Level
Low Occurrence Prob.
High Impact
c. catastrophic events such as floods, fires, or other disruptive events affecting key data centres in which data is stored may result in loss of data.
The WP8 team is made aware of the location of the relevant data centres and monitors the occurrence of catastrophic events near to the relevant locations.
Local data backups are maintained to mitigate the risk of catastrophic events leading to data loss.
Low Risk Level
Low Occurrence Prob.
Data is processed by the WP8 team, the ITSERR PMO and the PRIN 2022 “Resilient Septuagint” team.
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO

Metadata in which information protected by copyright is included is not made available, unless proper rights have been explicitly obtained from the copyright holder. Privacy and confidentiality: metadata that contains personally identifiable information related to people who participated in interviews, or to the ITSERR project, or whose personal information has been collected specifically for the ITSERR project, and not as a part of already published material, is not made accessible.
Transmission of data is encrypted, and access is managed through the D4Science share features. This ensures security, reliability, efficiency, accessibility.
The data is structured by the WP8 team with the collaboration of the PRIN 2022 “Resilient Septuagint” project. They are stored and versioned on Google Drive and on a dedicated GitHub repository.
Decision makers are those identified by the ITSERR DMP.

textListValues.textListValue: Structured data (HTML, JSON, TEX, XML, RDF)

field.textValue: Data is checked and validated by the WP8 team before publication.
In the short and medium term, data is curated and maintained by the WP8 team.

Only data relevant to the contextualization, support, and functioning of uBIQUity will be shared. This mostly includes textual materials, spatial or chronological metadata, software chunks, collected or produced by the WP8 team for the purpose of uBIQUity, and project deliverables.
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO

The data is collected by the WP8 team, with the collaboration of the ITSERR PMO, ITSERR service providers, the PRIN 2022 “Resilient Septuagint” project (for the Ancient Greek corpus), and WP4 of ITSERR (for the Latin corpus). Collection is carried out through transcription and adaptation of publicly available data, followed by structured encoding, critical revision, and semantic enrichment.

uBIQUity works on ancient religious texts in three languages: Ancient Greek, Latin, and Arabic. The Greek and Latin data come from printed reference editions of Bible versions and patristic works. Most of these editions had already been digitized before the uBIQUity project began; some, however, were digitized by the uBIQUity team via OCR (for example, P. Sabatier [ed.], Bibliorum Sacrorum latinae versiones antiquae seu Vetus Italica, 3 vols., vol. III, Reims: Reginaldus Florentain, 1751). The Arabic corpus includes over 150 digitalized documents. These comprise the Qurʾān, Qurʾānic commentaries (tafāsīr) dating from the 8th to the 15th century, and both canonical and non-canonical ḥadīth collections from the Sunni and Shia traditions. The texts are sourced from the Open Islamicate Texts Initiative (OpenITI), an open-access digital library of Arabic texts. Data used for the design of uBIQUity also comes from interviews, analysis of software documentation, analysis of scientific literature, and the work of the WP8 team researchers.

Periodic (2 months) backups are made locally, on an external hard drive. Backups are maintained for 2 backup cycles (4 months). At the end of the project, backed up data is deleted, once the primary data (and metadata) is validated.

Collected corpora have been checked and reprocessed for quality and are stored in structured formats such as XML-TEI, XML-OSIS, XML-TEI-EpiDoc, and plain text, depending on the nature of the source. Encoding follows community standards (e.g. TEI P5, OSIS, EpiDoc) with project-specific schema constraints to ensure structural consistency. Authority files (VIAF, Wikidata) are used systematically for the normalization and disambiguation of named entities. Data is reviewed and re-encoded by WP8 team members with the collaboration of the PRIN 2022 “Resilient Septuagint” team. Validation is carried out across all formats using schema-aware tools (e.g. oXygen). A versioning system (e.g. Git) is employed to track changes, ensure data integrity, and maintain transparency over time. An appropriate open and interoperable publication format (e.g. JSON) will be adopted based on uBIQUity platform requirements.
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO
WP8 team, ITSERR PMO
Project standards are adopted according to the ITSERR DMP.
- Textual documents (dataset). - uBIQUity retrieval system and user interface (software). - Quantitative experimental results. - Audiovisual training materials.
Data with confidential/sensitive nature is identified at project level by the ITSERR DMP. For the scope of WP8, data of confidential/sensitive nature is data with personally identifiable information.

Data are stored locally and synchronized with cloud-based repositories (Google Drive and GitHub) to ensure redundancy, version control, and collaborative access. Long-term preservation is guaranteed through deposition in Zenodo, which assigns DOIs and ensures persistent, citable access. All data are saved in open, standard formats (XML-TEI, XML-OSIS, XML-EpiDoc, plain text, JSON), with structured organization in modular folder hierarchies and consistent naming conventions. Metadata is stored either embedded (XML) or in structured files (e.g. JSON, Markdown) and enriched through authority files such as VIAF and Wikidata. Version control is managed through Git, ensuring traceability and integrity of all changes. Data are made openly accessible unless legal, ethical, or copyright constraints require restricted access, in which case appropriate limitations are applied.

Data with sensitive or confidential information which has become irrelevant for the contextualization, support and understanding of uBIQUity is destroyed at the end of the project. Data for which copyright permission has been not granted, or has been withdrawn, is deleted upon the end of the project or upon request, whatever comes first.

uBIQUity integrates within the software some pre-existing open access online databases, such as Corpus Corporum and Open Islamicate Texts Initiative (OpenITI), and linguistic resources from LiLa: Linking Latin. For the bibliographical references it includes links to Zotero library.
N/A (data which cannot be shared is not made public).

Third parties are allowed the possibility to cite and reuse the data, within the limits of the CC BY-ND license. Permission is granted for mixing, adapting, and reusing data for academic or non-academic purposes, provided that the fact that data was modified is clearly stated.

Our dataset has been collected, processed, reviewed and/or produced by biblical scholars, patrologists and specialists in Islam of the uBIQUity team, in constant cooperation with IT experts and according to the scientific quality criteria set for ITSERR and RESILIENCE, in machine readable form. Research data enriched with the reference bibliography, templates, training materials and other technical documentation have been saved in a coherent and well-ordered manner in specific shared folders on GDrive and on Zotero, so that they are easier to find, understand and reuse. Regarding biblical texts, the uBIQUity dataset embraces their textual plurality by including some information from the apparatus of their reference editions, which will be made available within the limits posed by copyright.
Main risks to data security are: accidental leakage due to mishandling of data; accidental data deletion; catastrophic events.

uBIQUity relies on established disciplinary standards for textual encoding and scholarly data representation, including TEI P5, EpiDoc (for epigraphic and manuscript materials), and OSIS (for scriptural texts). Where applicable, authority control is ensured through VIAF and Wikidata. For specific data types such as structured variant records and parser outputs, no fully consolidated disciplinary standard currently exists. These will be documented with detailed schemas and designed to facilitate future conversion or alignment with emerging standards in the field of textual scholarship and digital philology.
